# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Small Legal-Tech Tool for Family Lawyers

> **Mode C — terse brief.** Assumptions table below covers every defaulted field. Confirm or adjust any row and I will re-run the affected stages.

---

## 0. Assumptions

| Field | Assumed Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Sector | `b2b-saas` — software tool sold to professional practitioners | Inferred from "legal-tech tool for family lawyers" |
| Outcome | Free-trial signups or direct subscriptions from family-law practitioners | Defaulted — not stated in brief |
| Audience | Family lawyers at small-to-mid practices (solo practitioners, 2–10 person firms), primarily English-speaking markets | Inferred from "family lawyers"; geography unspecified |
| Competition (specifics) | LexisNexis named. Other dominant legal-research incumbents treated at category level per industry-peer rule | Brief named LexisNexis only |
| Existing channels / traction | Starting from zero (no newsletter, community, or follower base assumed) | Default under Mode C |
| Bottleneck | Trust — practitioners choosing legal tools based on peer recommendation and reliability, not awareness | Default 2025–2026 underdog |
| Time window | 60-day rolling campaign, then recurring cadence | Default under Mode C |
| Capacity | 6h/week | Default under Mode C |
| Budget (user) | Under €1k/month | Inferred from "outspent 100x" framing; exact figure unknown |
| Budget (competitor) | LexisNexis spend estimated at 50–100× user spend based on brief statement | Brief stated 100x explicitly |

*"Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table and I will re-run the affected stages."*

---

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5+ concepts across archetypes)

> **Anti-fabrication and industry-peer rule applied throughout.** LexisNexis is named because the brief named it. No other legal-research or legal-tech platform is named. Where peer incumbents are referenced, escape-hatch phrasing is used: "LexisNexis-class tools," "the other major research platforms," "the dominant legal-research incumbents."

---

### Concept A — "The Family Lawyer's Tool, Built by Someone Who Gets It"
**Archetype:** Founder-story arc  
**One-line thesis:** In a market where LexisNexis sells to law-firm procurement committees, a founder who speaks the language of the family court hallway becomes the product's most powerful distribution channel.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder-led LinkedIn + direct community outreach)  
**Authenticity hook:** The real story of why this tool was built — the specific frustration a family lawyer faces that LexisNexis does not solve (the billing model, the UX, the irrelevant search noise). That story, told in the founder's own words, is the ad that LexisNexis cannot buy.  
**Minimum viable version:** One 500-word LinkedIn post from the founder telling the origin story, published Monday morning, tagged to 5 family-law practitioners the founder actually knows. Track saves and DMs in 48 hours.

---

### Concept B — "The Real Cost" Counter-Narrative
**Archetype:** Counter-narrative / price-transparency play  
**One-line thesis:** LexisNexis charges what it charges because the buyer (the law firm partner) is not the user (the associate doing research at midnight). Surface the total cost — in money, in wasted time, in irrelevant results — and let family lawyers do the math themselves.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1–2 (SEO-first long-form content + earned media in bar association publications)  
**Authenticity hook:** A real, anonymized breakdown of what a solo family-law practice actually pays for LexisNexis annually vs. what it gets. Numbers the practitioner recognizes from their own invoice. Not a marketing estimate — a real receipt, if the founder or a friendly customer will share one.  
**Minimum viable version:** One 800-word article: "What family lawyers actually pay for [legal research platforms], broken down." Published on the product blog, submitted to one state bar association newsletter. Track organic search impressions in 30 days.

---

### Concept C — "Family Law Practice Forum" Community Build
**Archetype:** Community-build flywheel  
**One-line thesis:** Family lawyers are underserved by the generic legal-tech communities that cater to BigLaw. A tight, practitioner-specific forum becomes the product's distribution channel because the people who solve each other's problems become the people who recommend each other's tools.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (owned community — Slack or Circle)  
**Authenticity hook:** The community is not a product onboarding funnel. It is a practice-management forum where LexisNexis is just one of many topics discussed. Practitioners stay for the community; they trial the product because they trust the host.  
**Minimum viable version:** Open a private Slack workspace. Invite 10 family-law practitioners the founder knows personally. Let it run for 14 days before any product mention. Track whether unsolicited product questions arise.

---

### Concept D — "The 30-Minute Case Prep" Earned-Media Stunt
**Archetype:** Earned-media stunt / demonstration play  
**One-line thesis:** Show, in public, the speed gap between a family-law task done in the product vs. the same task done in a LexisNexis-class tool. The demo is the press pitch.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (YouTube / LinkedIn video + earned media outreach to legal-tech press)  
**Authenticity hook:** A real family-law scenario (custody modification research, QDRO language lookup, jurisdiction-specific statute check) timed on screen. No edits. No voice-over marketing. Raw footage. The gap in time is the argument.  
**Minimum viable version:** One 10-minute unedited screen recording. Post to YouTube with full timestamps. Send to two legal-tech journalists with the hook: "We timed family-law research on LexisNexis and on our tool. The gap surprised us." Track press pickups and video views in 14 days.

---

### Concept E — "State Bar Insider" Referral Coalition
**Archetype:** Coalition play / referral flywheel  
**One-line thesis:** Every state has a family law section of the bar association. Those sections have newsletters, CLEs, and listservs. Becoming the recommended tool of one family law section is worth more than any paid campaign.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1–2 (coalition outreach + CLE sponsorship at near-zero cost)  
**Authenticity hook:** The tool solves a real family-law-specific workflow problem (not a generic legal research problem) that the bar section leadership can verify by trialing it themselves. The endorsement is earned, not bought.  
**Minimum viable version:** Identify the family law sections in 3 states where the founder has contacts. Offer a free 30-minute CLE webinar: "Practice management for family law in 2026." No product pitch in the CLE itself. Product mention only in the bio. Track section newsletter pickups and post-webinar trial signups.

---

### Concept F — "Open Playbook" Demand-Capture SEO
**Archetype:** Demand capture / resource flywheel  
**One-line thesis:** Family lawyers are Googling specific procedural questions at 11pm. Ranking for those queries — not "legal research software" but "QDRO language family court [state]," "custody modification petition template," "child support calculation [state] 2026" — means the product is already open in the browser when the practitioner realizes they need a better tool.  
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2–3 (SEO content + non-brand search capture)  
**Authenticity hook:** The content is genuinely useful — real templates, real checklists, real jurisdiction-specific guidance — not content-marketing fluff. If a family lawyer bookmarks it, the product wins.  
**Minimum viable version:** Publish 3 long-tail jurisdiction-specific resource pages (e.g., "[State] child support modification checklist 2026"). Track Google Search Console impressions and clicks at 30 days.

---

> **Stage 1 selection prompt (Mode C auto-proceeding):** Because this is a skill run producing a full deliverable, I am proceeding with **Concept A (Founder-Story Arc)** as the selected concept — it scores highest given the trust bottleneck, categorical spend asymmetry, and the structural advantage that LexisNexis's scale prevents it from matching. However, all three alternative campaign shapes in Section 7 draw from different concepts above, so the user retains a real menu.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

**Concept A — "The Family Lawyer's Tool, Built by Someone Who Gets It"** (Founder-story arc, Tier 1 primary)

Rationale: At 100x spend asymmetry, the founder's authentic voice is the one asset LexisNexis cannot manufacture. The trust bottleneck in professional legal software is best attacked by credibility signals, not reach. Founder-led content on LinkedIn, amplified into the family-law practitioner community, is the asymmetric lever.

The other concepts (B, D, F) are folded into the Three Alternative Shapes in Section 7 so the user can choose or combine.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**Your asymmetry is categorical (100:1+).** This means paid media is structurally off the table as a primary channel — LexisNexis can out-bid you on every keyword, every platform, and every audience segment without noticing the spend. Your leverage is the channels where money does not compound: founder credibility, practitioner peer trust, earned media, and community relationships that the sales team of a $2B platform cannot replicate at your price point.

### Preconditions Check (scored against the Hungarian case factors)

1. **Credible insider / founder-story equivalent.** Does the founder have a real connection to family law practice — either as a former practitioner, a close family member in the field, or a documented year-long engagement with the user community? *Score pending — assume yes for plan, flag as must-verify.*

2. **Accumulated grievance or unmet demand.** Is there documented frustration with LexisNexis pricing, UX, or relevance for family-law-specific workflows? This is highly plausible (family law is a niche LexisNexis serves as a secondary market relative to BigLaw and corporate litigation). *Likely yes — score 1.*

3. **Consolidated challenger field.** Is this tool the one clear alternative for family-law-specific workflows, or one of 20 generic legal-research tools? If it is purpose-built for family law and there is no other direct family-law-specific tool, this precondition holds. *Score pending — must verify niche specificity.*

4. **Felt pain, not abstract pain.** Does every target (solo family-law practitioner) experience the cost of LexisNexis directly in their practice P&L? Yes — billing is per-seat and visible on the invoice. The pain is felt, not distributed. *Score 1.*

5. **Threshold-rewarding market.** Legal-tech referrals are heavily word-of-mouth through bar sections, CLEs, and practitioner listservs. Once the tool is "what the family law bar recommends," adoption compounds. *Score 1.*

6. **Incumbent overplaying its hand.** LexisNexis's marketing saturates generic legal-research channels with enterprise-positioned messaging. A tool that is explicitly built for family lawyers, priced for solo practices, and voiced by a real practitioner-facing founder is counter-positioned by default. *Score 1.*

**Preconditions score: 4/6 (pending verification of #1 and #3).**

**Verdict:** Proceed with the organic-first playbook. Flag preconditions #1 (credible founder story) and #3 (consolidated niche) as campaign sub-goals to be built and demonstrated in the first 30 days. If the founder does not yet have deep personal credibility with the family-law community, building that credibility *is* the first campaign action — not content production.

---

## 3a. Message-Market-Fit Gate

Three yes/no questions. "I don't know" is treated as "no."

**Q1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Have 10+ family-law practitioners paid for, signed up for, or directly asked (without prompting) for this tool?
*Assumed: Unknown — defaulting to borderline. If the tool has any paying customers, this is yes.*

**Q2 — Language signal:** Can the founder name a specific pain that family lawyers articulate in their own words before being pitched?
*Assumed: Likely yes, given the brief implies product-market context ("I run a legal-tech tool"). Treating as yes for plan.*

**Q3 — Close signal:** If the founder asked 5 family lawyers to commit today, would 3+ say yes?
*Assumed: Unknown — borderline.*

**MMF Verdict: 2/3 — Borderline.**

The plan proceeds but with a mandatory 1–2 week validation cycle inserted as a pre-campaign task:

- **If Q1 is yes (10+ paying/signed-up customers):** Skip the validation cycle and run the full plan.
- **If Q1 is no:** Before running the channel stack, do 5 structured 20-minute customer-discovery calls with family lawyers. Objective: confirm the pain in their words (Q2) and test willingness to pay (Q3). Run a free-trial or paid-waitlist test with 10 personal outreach emails. Do not launch public content until Q1 is confirmed.

*The organic-first playbook scales trust. It cannot manufacture demand for a product practitioners do not already want. Running a full campaign before Q1 is confirmed burns the founder-credibility capital that the playbook depends on.*

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function: Trust compounding.**

Rationale: The bottleneck is not that family lawyers cannot find legal-tech tools. LexisNexis is known. The bottleneck is that switching from a known incumbent requires trust in the alternative — trust that the tool will not fail at 11pm before a hearing, that the company will not disappear, that other practitioners use it. Distribution amplifies trust signals; trust must come first.

**Sector rider: B2B SaaS applied.**

The B2B SaaS rider weights toward: founder LinkedIn + demand capture (long-tail SEO) + referral/coalition. It de-weights: consumer UGC flywheels, broadcast social, political-ground-game tactics. Conflict check: rider biases toward founder-LinkedIn (trust compounding) AND demand capture (search). Both apply here. Running both in parallel with a time-phased priority (trust compounding first, demand capture layered in weeks 3–4).

**Categorical asymmetry (100:1) → Tier 1 and 2 only. No broad cold-paid Tier 4.**

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Weekly Effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Founder LinkedIn (personal, not company page) | Tier 1 | 2h/week | 2 posts/week. Founder voice, practitioner-specific problems. No marketing language. |
| 2 | Direct outreach — family-law practitioners the founder knows | Tier 1 | 1h/week | 5 personal emails or DMs per week. Not blast — personal. |
| 3 | Bar association family-law sections (3 target states) | Tier 1 | 1h/week | Relationship-building with section leadership. CLE pitch prep in weeks 3–4. |
| 4 | Long-tail SEO content (family-law-specific resource pages) | Tier 2 | 1.5h/week | 1 long-form resource page per 2 weeks. Jurisdiction-specific, genuinely useful. |
| 5 | Earned media (legal-tech press + bar newsletters) | Tier 2 | 0.5h/week | Targeted outreach. Specific targets named in Section 8 action list. |

**Total: 6h/week — fits stated capacity ceiling.**

No Tier 3 or Tier 4 channels at categorical asymmetry. If Q1 confirms paying customers and the budget picture sharpens, revisit in 60 days.

---

## 5. 70/30 (or 80/20) Allocation

**Allocation: 100% organic (Tier 1 + 2), 0% paid.**

At categorical asymmetry (100:1), the 80/20 split is not the right frame. The correct frame is: paid channels are a gift to LexisNexis. Every dollar spent on Google Ads or LinkedIn Ads competes on an auction where LexisNexis sets the floor. The organic-first playbook is not a budget constraint — it is the strategic choice. Paid channels are re-evaluated only when:

1. A specific organic piece of content shows organic traction (saves, DMs, shares — not likes).
2. The campaign has confirmed MMF (Q1 signal).
3. The budget picture is clearer and a specific boost target (not a broad cold audience) is identified.

Until all three conditions are met: 100% organic.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

### LexisNexis

**(a) What they saturate:**
LexisNexis saturates: enterprise procurement-targeted display advertising, law school relationship programs (brand imprinting before practitioners choose their own tools), conference sponsorships at large bar association events (ABA, state bars with >5,000 members), generic "legal research platform" and "case law search" paid search keywords, high-production-value thought leadership whitepapers positioned for law-firm management, and LinkedIn ads targeted at law firm partners and managing attorneys (not the practitioners doing the work).

Their visual register is: institutional, polished, dark-navy and gold, credential-heavy, enterprise-proof-point-led. Their emotional register is: authority and risk-reduction ("trusted by [N] of the AmLaw 100"). Their message trope is: reliability, comprehensiveness, and enterprise security. They almost never speak directly to the solo family-law practitioner.

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
LexisNexis's institutional positioning means it cannot credibly speak to the practitioner who is billing 40 hours a week and needs a tool that works in their actual workflow — not in the abstract workflow that an enterprise procurement slide deck describes. Their scale is the tell: the product is built for a procurement committee, not for the lawyer at 11pm before a custody hearing.

Your refusal to imitate their register — no navy-and-gold brand decks, no "trusted by [N]%" proof points, no enterprise RFP language — is the positioning. You show up in the spaces LexisNexis cannot: the family-law listserv, the state bar section newsletter, the 10-minute demo that a real solo practitioner shares with a colleague because it actually saved them time.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"The legal-research tool that was built for the family court hallway, not the AmLaw procurement committee."

*(Hold this line across every piece of content. Every post, every email, every CLE bio should reinforce it — explicitly or structurally.)*

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape 1 — Founder-Story First (Selected Concept A)

**Description:** The founder publishes two LinkedIn posts per week for 60 days. Each post addresses a specific family-law practitioner pain point: billing opacity, irrelevant search results for family-law statutes, the UX gap between a tool built for BigLaw and one used in family court. The posts are in the founder's voice — specific, honest, occasionally uncomfortable about the competitor landscape. No marketing language. Direct calls-to-action are rare and earned.

**Self / Us / Now structure for flagship content:**
- *Self:* The founder's specific moment of frustration with the existing tools (a real incident — a failed search, a billing surprise, a UX cliff-edge before a hearing).
- *Us:* The shared reality of solo and small-firm family lawyers who face the same gap every week.
- *Now:* A specific, time-bound ask — "Try it on your next case prep and tell me what you find."

**Tradeoffs:**
- Works fastest if the founder is already respected in the family-law community or is willing to be visible and specific.
- Fails if the founder is uncomfortable being public, or if the posts read as marketing rather than practitioner-to-practitioner conversation.
- Requires 60 days of consistent output before compounding begins.

**Best for:** Founder with personal credibility in the family-law space and comfort with personal LinkedIn posting.

---

### Shape 2 — Earned-Media and Demonstration First (Concept D + B)

**Description:** The campaign opens with one newsworthy demonstration: a timed, unedited screen recording of a real family-law research task completed in the product vs. the same task completed in a LexisNexis-class tool. The recording is sent to 3 named legal-tech journalists and 2 state bar association newsletter editors with a specific pitch hook. If any pick it up, the campaign accelerates. If none do, the recording is published on YouTube and LinkedIn as organic content.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Has a higher ceiling than Shape 1 if a journalist picks it up (one article can produce 200+ trial signups).
- Has a harder floor — if no press pickup occurs, the campaign needs Shape 1 content to carry it.
- Requires a real, compelling time gap in the demonstration. If the gap is not dramatic, the stunt falls flat.

**Best for:** Founder who has a genuinely faster or better workflow story to demonstrate, and confidence that the demonstration will hold up under scrutiny.

---

### Shape 3 — Search-Capture and Resource Flywheel First (Concept F)

**Description:** The campaign builds a library of jurisdiction-specific family-law resources — templates, checklists, calculation guides — that rank for the specific long-tail queries family lawyers are already Googling. The product is introduced as the context, not the pitch. Over 60 days, 6–8 resource pages are published and submitted to bar association newsletters as free practitioner resources.

**Tradeoffs:**
- Lowest ceiling in 60 days (SEO compounds slowly), but highest floor — the resources remain useful and discoverable indefinitely.
- Does not require the founder to be visible or credible in the community in advance.
- Requires genuine knowledge of family-law workflows to produce content that practitioners bookmark. Generic content will not rank and will not convert.

**Best for:** Founder with deep family-law workflow knowledge but limited personal community presence, or a team that can produce genuinely useful jurisdiction-specific content at 1 page per 2 weeks.

---

**Recommendation:** Begin with Shape 1 (weeks 1–2) to establish the founder's voice and build the first trust signals. Layer in Shape 2 (the demonstration stunt) in week 3 once there is at least one compelling organic post to point to. Add Shape 3 resources as the capacity allows. None of these shapes conflicts; they compound.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Capacity ceiling: 6h/week. All actions summed and trimmed to fit.**

### Pre-campaign (before Day 1): Validation gate

If Q1 (10+ paying/signed-up practitioners) is not yet confirmed:
- Send 10 personal emails to family lawyers in the founder's existing network. Subject: "Would you try [product name] on your next case prep — 30 minutes, honest feedback?" Track replies and trial signups. This is the MMF confirmation step.
- Target: 3 trial commitments before launching public content.
- Effort: 2h (write templates + send). Not counted against weekly ceiling.

---

### Week 1

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write and publish Founder LinkedIn Post #1: the origin story (Self/Us/Now structure). Specific incident, no marketing language, tagged to 3–5 family-law practitioners the founder knows personally. | 1.5h | Founder | 5+ saves, 3+ DMs within 48h |
| Send 5 personal outreach emails to family-law practitioners the founder knows. Not a product pitch — an invitation to a conversation about their practice workflow. | 1h | Founder | 2+ replies |
| Identify target earned-media outlets: research family-law-specific publications and bar association newsletters in 3 target states. Produce a list of 10 targets with editor names and contact channels. | 1h | Founder | Ranked list of 10 targets with contact info by Friday |
| Begin bar-section outreach: email family-law section chair in 1 target state. Offer a free 30-minute CLE webinar on practice management (no product pitch in the CLE). | 0.5h | Founder | Reply within 1 week |

**Week 1 total: 4h. Within ceiling.**

---

### Week 2

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write and publish Founder LinkedIn Post #2: a specific family-law workflow pain the tool solves, described in practitioner language. No CTAs until the post earns organic engagement. | 1.5h | Founder | Saves and shares > Post #1, or 1 DM from a non-connected practitioner |
| Send 5 personal outreach emails (new batch — different practitioners). | 1h | Founder | 2+ replies |
| Write Earned-Media Pitch #1: 2-sentence hook matched to 1 named target outlet (e.g., the family-law section newsletter editor identified in Week 1). Send on Tuesday morning. | 1h | Founder | Reply or newsletter inquiry within 10 days |
| Produce draft of Resource Page #1: jurisdiction-specific checklist (e.g., "[State] child support modification checklist 2026"). Optimized for a specific long-tail query. | 1.5h | Founder | Draft ready for publish in Week 3 |

**Week 2 total: 5h. Within ceiling.**

---

### Week 3

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish Resource Page #1 (from Week 2 draft). Submit to 1 bar association newsletter as a free practitioner resource. | 0.5h | Founder | Newsletter editor reply or publication |
| Write and publish Founder LinkedIn Post #3: reaction or follow-up to engagement from Posts #1 and #2. If a DM surfaced a compelling practitioner story (with permission), center the post on it. | 1.5h | Founder | 1 unsolicited share or repost from a practitioner |
| Record the demonstration video (Concept D): timed, unedited, real family-law research task. Publish to YouTube and LinkedIn with timestamps. No edits. | 2h | Founder | 100+ YouTube views or 3 DMs asking about the product within 72h |
| Send Earned-Media Pitch #2 to a legal-tech journalist (named target from Week 1 list). Hook: "We timed family-law research on LexisNexis and our tool. The gap surprised us. Here's the raw footage." | 0.5h | Founder | Reply or coverage inquiry |

**Week 3 total: 4.5h. Within ceiling.**

---

### Week 4

| Action | Effort | Owner | Success Signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write and publish Founder LinkedIn Post #4: behind-the-scenes of what building a tool for family lawyers revealed about how LexisNexis-class tools are architected (vs. how solo practitioners actually work). | 1.5h | Founder | 1 share from outside founder's existing network |
| Send 5 personal outreach emails (new batch). | 1h | Founder | 2+ trial signups from the 60-day campaign batch |
| Follow up on CLE webinar offer to bar section (Week 1 outreach). Schedule date if reply received. | 0.5h | Founder | Webinar scheduled for weeks 5–6 |
| Begin Resource Page #2 draft (different jurisdiction or different workflow area). | 1h | Founder | Draft ready by end of week |
| Review Week 1–4 LinkedIn data. Identify the 1 post with highest saves-to-impressions ratio. That is the boost candidate — do not boost yet. Log it for the lift-test plan. | 0.5h | Founder | Identified top-performing post |

**Week 4 total: 4.5h. Within ceiling.**

---

**Cuts from draft plan (exceeded 6h/week):** Removed: community Slack launch (moved to month 2 if month 1 earns 5+ engaged trial users), Twitter/X presence (low organic signal for B2B legal in 2026), paid-directory listing campaigns (not appropriate at categorical asymmetry without MMF confirmation). These are the correct cuts — if capacity opens up, add the community node first.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**At categorical asymmetry (100:1), no broad cold-paid creative is produced here.** Explanation: At this spend ratio, every dollar on Google Search Ads competes on keywords where LexisNexis sets the bid floor. Every dollar on LinkedIn Ads reaches an audience that LexisNexis is already retargeting with enterprise proof points. Cold-paid at this asymmetry is a subsidy to the incumbent's remarketing lists.

**The 24–48h organic traction gate (standing rule for when paid is revisited):**
Do not boost any post until it has demonstrated genuine organic signal: saves (not likes), DMs, shares, or thread-depth comments from practitioners who are not in the founder's personal network. A post with 200 impressions and 12 saves is a boost candidate. A post with 2,000 impressions and 150 likes is not.

**If at day 60 the campaign has:**
- 3+ posts with saves-to-impressions ratio >3%
- MMF confirmed (Q1 signal: 10+ paying/trial users)
- Budget to allocate: at least €500/month

**Then and only then:** Run a warm retargeting campaign to website visitors (not cold audiences). Creative direction: the top-performing organic post, adapted minimally. Do not produce new creative for paid — boost what the audience already validated. Audience: website visitors in the past 30 days only. Budget: €200/month test. Duration: 30 days. Then run the lift test before scaling.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template 5 — Organic Source Attribution (zero-budget, directional)**

Because the campaign is 100% organic in Phase 1, the lift test measures which organic channels are driving trial signups and conversion, rather than comparing paid to control.

**Hypothesis:** Founder LinkedIn posts drive higher trial signup conversion rate per unique visitor than resource-page SEO traffic in the first 60 days, because the trust signal from a named founder is stronger than anonymous content discovery for a practitioner-category purchase decision.

**Setup:**
- UTM-tag every content piece: `utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=founder-post&utm_campaign=organic-first-60d` for LinkedIn; `utm_source=organic-search&utm_medium=resource-page` for SEO content; `utm_source=referral&utm_medium=bar-newsletter` for earned media.
- Track: unique visitors per source → trial signup rate per source → paid conversion rate per source (if any).
- Duration: 60 days from campaign launch.
- Minimum sample per channel: 100 unique visitors before drawing conclusions (flag if a channel does not reach 100 visitors — do not cut it, just extend the window).

**Decision threshold:**
- Top channel by trial-signup conversion rate → double effort allocation in month 2.
- Bottom channel (<20% of top channel's conversion rate, with ≥100 visitors) → drop from active investment. Redirect time to top channel.
- If LinkedIn founder posts and earned-media referrals both outperform SEO in month 1: maintain both and deprioritize SEO content production until month 3.

**Caveat:** Template 5 is directional only. It measures correlation, not causation. A practitioner who read 3 LinkedIn posts, one bar newsletter mention, and then Googled the product name will be attributed to the last touch. Use it to allocate effort, not to make causal claims about channel ROI.

**Incremental metric:** Trial signups per unique visitor per channel. Not impressions. Not follower growth. Not platform-reported engagement rate.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track

| Metric | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Trial signups from each UTM source | Direct signal of conversion, channel by channel |
| LinkedIn post saves (not likes) | Saves indicate "I want to return to this" — the practitioner is considering the product |
| LinkedIn post DMs from non-connected practitioners | Unsolicited outreach = trust breakthrough |
| Shares and reposts from practitioners not in founder's network | Organic amplification outside the existing audience |
| Bar section newsletter pickups | Tier 1 coalition traction signal |
| CLE webinar attendance and post-webinar trial signups | Conversion from relationship to product trial |
| Earned-media mentions (press, newsletters) | Coverage that persists and compounds |
| Trial-to-paid conversion rate | The real MMF signal |
| Customer-discovery call outcomes (if running validation cycle) | MMF confirmation before scaling |

### Ignore

| Metric | Why it lies |
|---|---|
| Total LinkedIn impressions | Impressions above ~5 touches do not persuade; they are reach noise |
| Follower count | A practitioner who follows but never saves or clicks is not a prospect |
| Raw likes | The cheapest engagement signal; does not predict trial intent |
| CPM / CPC (if any paid is eventually run) | Platform-attributed cost metrics, not incremental lift |
| Platform-reported ROAS (if any paid is eventually run) | Platforms are graded on attributed conversions; brand-keyword traffic converts regardless of ads (see Blake, Nosko & Tadelis 2015). Never accept platform ROAS as proof of incremental lift. |
| Website pageviews without conversion tracking | Pageviews from people who will never trial the product are noise |
| Google Analytics sessions (without UTM filtering) | Too noisy without source segmentation |
| Email open rate | Directional at best; Apple Mail Privacy Protection has made it nearly meaningless |

---

*"Confirm or adjust any row in the Assumptions table (Section 0) and I will re-run the affected stages."*
